title: Three-dimensional papercut shadowbox
subtitle: Stacking cut layers on foam mounts to build depth into a paper scene
excerpt: A papercut shadowbox builds a three-dimensional scene by stacking several separately cut paper layers, each raised from the previous on foam adhesive pads. The viewer looks down through the layers and sees different depths of cut detail. An advanced project that rewards patient, precise cutting: each layer must be designed to align with the others and to look correct when viewed from a single angle.
sourceNotes: Three-dimensional paper sculpture traditions appear across multiple craft cultures. The Western shadowbox format, stacked layers in a deep frame, draws on the paper-engineering tradition documented in early twentieth-century German and Czech paper-toy and toy-theatre design. The stacked-layer technique itself is straightforward and widely documented in twentieth-century craft publications.

glossaryTerms.definition: A deep-sided frame that encloses a three-dimensional paper scene. The depth of the box allows multiple layers of papercuts to be stacked, each at a different depth, creating parallax and shadow effects.

The alignment of multiple cut layers so that their intended features line up correctly when stacked. Good registration means the horizon lines, scale, and focal points read as a coherent scene; poor registration makes the layers look misaligned when viewed head-on.

The impression of three-dimensional depth produced by stacked flat layers. Moving the viewing angle slightly causes near layers to shift relative to far layers, producing genuine depth cues even though each layer is a flat sheet.

projectSchedule.label: Day 1, design all layers and cut Layer 1 (foreground)
Day 2, cut Layers 2 and 3 (mid-ground and background)

attrs.title: Cutting tools
attrs.body: Use a fresh scalpel blade for each layer. Blunt blades tear the paper fibres and make curved cuts ragged.

content.text: A three-layer
shadowbox
uses three cut sheets of different weights or colours: a pale background, a mid-toned middle layer, and a dark or richly detailed foreground. The
parallax depth
comes from the 5 mm height of the foam adhesive dots between each layer. Good
layer registration
is the technical challenge: the three layers must line up horizontally and vertically so the scene reads coherently.
What you need

items.name: Cartridge paper, 220 gsm, three sheets, the same size (A5 is manageable for a first attempt)
items.substitutions: For extra depth contrast: use different paper colours or weights for each layer

items.name: Foam adhesive dots, 5 mm height
items.substitutions: Foam adhesive squares cut to size; two stacked adhesive dots for extra height

content.text: Designing the layers (Day 1)

Choose a simple scene: a forest silhouette works well. Background layer: a simple moon and hill line cut from the lightest paper. Mid-ground: tree trunks with some branches. Foreground: the most detailed layer, dense foliage, plants, or animals.

Draw all three layers on paper first at actual size. Check that the horizon line is at the same height on all three layers. Each layer must retain a connected border at its edges to hold together when cut, plan this as part of the design.
Cutting (Days 1 and 2)
Transfer each design to its paper sheet by tracing or lightbox transfer.
Cut interior negative spaces first, working inward. Cut the outer silhouette last.
Cut each layer on a fresh scalpel blade. Work slowly through fine details.
Assembly (Day 3)
Lay the background flat in the back of the frame or on a mounting board.
Apply foam dots at three or four points to the back of the mid-ground layer. Press the mid-ground onto the background, aligning the horizon. The foam raises the mid-ground 5 mm above the background.
Apply a second set of foam dots to the back of the foreground layer and press onto the mid-ground. Check the alignment from the front before the adhesive sets fully.
Common mistakes

items.symptom: The layers look misaligned when viewed head-on
items.cause: The horizon lines or border edges were not consistent across all three designs.
items.fix: Place all three cut layers flat on the table before assembling. Align by eye and tape lightly together to check registration before applying the foam dots.

items.symptom: The paper tears when cutting fine details
items.cause: Paper is too thin; blade too blunt.
items.fix: Use 220 gsm cartridge for all layers, heavier paper holds fine cut details better. Change the blade.
